(known as the *Liga BBVA* for sponsorship reasons) was the 80th season of [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") since its establishment. The campaign began on 28 August 2010, and concluded on 21 May 2011. A total of 20 teams contested the league, 17 of which already partook in the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga"), and three of which were promoted from the [Segunda División](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2009–10 Segunda División"). In addition, a new match ball – the Nike Total 90 Tracer – served as the official ball for all matches.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The **2010–11 La Liga** (known as the *Liga BBVA* for sponsorship reasons) was the 80th season of [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") since its establishment. The campaign began on 28 August 2010, and concluded on 21 May 2011. A total of 20 teams contested the league, 17 of which already partook in the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga"), and three of which were promoted from the [Segunda División](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2009–10 Segunda División"). In addition, a new match ball – the Nike Total 90 Tracer – served as the official ball for all matches.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Defending champions [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona") secured a third consecutive and overall 21st La Liga title following a 1–1 draw with [Levante](/wiki/Levante_UD "Levante UD") on 11 May 2011.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The result gave Barcelona a six-point lead with two matches remaining which, combined with their superior head-to-head record against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F."), ensured that they would finish top of the table.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Barcelona led the table since defeating Real Madrid 5–0 on 23 November 2010. From that point onwards, they lost only one match en route to winning the title.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Barcelona's [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") won La Liga's [Award for Best Player](/wiki/LFP_Awards "LFP Awards") for a third straight year.

The season was again dominated by Barcelona and Real Madrid, with second-place Madrid 21 points ahead of third-place [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Having also faced off in the [Champions League](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_UEFA_Champions_League "2010–11 UEFA Champions League") semi-finals and the [Copa del Rey final](/wiki/2011_Copa_del_Rey_final "2011 Copa del Rey final"), the top two [rivals](/wiki/El_Cl%C3%A1sico "El Clásico") met four times over seventeen days, for a total of five meetings this season.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2010%E2%80%9311_La_Liga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Teams")]

[Real Valladolid](/wiki/Real_Valladolid "Real Valladolid"), [CD Tenerife](/wiki/CD_Tenerife "CD Tenerife") and [Xerez CD](/wiki/Xerez_CD "Xerez CD") were relegated to the [Segunda División](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2010–11 Segunda División") after finishing the [2009–10 season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga") in the bottom three places. Tenerife and Xerez made their immediate return to the second level after just one year in the Spanish top flight, while Valladolid ended a three-year tenure in La Liga.

The relegated teams were replaced by [2009–10 Segunda División](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2009–10 Segunda División") champions [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad "Real Sociedad") from [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián"), runners-up [Hércules CF](/wiki/H%C3%A9rcules_CF "Hércules CF") from [Alicante](/wiki/Alicante "Alicante") and [Levante UD](/wiki/Levante_UD "Levante UD") from [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia,_Spain "Valencia, Spain"). Hércules returned to the highest Spanish football league for the first time after 13 years, while Real Sociedad and Levante terminated their second-level status after three and two years, respectively.
